skill.name: project-testing
skill.description: Custom testing guidance for project-specific unit, integration, and end-to-end test patterns, fixtures, cleanup, naming, and coverage targets.
skill.summary: Guide project teams to create isolated, maintainable tests with fixtures, mocks, and coverage expectations.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but review found false positives. The flagged lines are Markdown inline code paths, test naming examples, table text, and words containing short substrings; no executable commands, cryptographic code, prompt injection, network access, or data exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Inline Code Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged inline backtick text as Ruby or shell command execution. These lines contain Markdown code formatting for test paths and naming examples, not executable code.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required. Keep these as Markdown examples unless marketplace policy requires avoiding inline backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are visible Markdown documentation and do not appear inside an executable script. No command interpreter, shell invocation, or user input execution path exists in the skill file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Example Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The Python fixture block was included in the command-execution matches, but it only imports sample fixtures and defines an empty illustrative test function.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required. If desired, label the block as illustrative documentation for clarity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The block is fenced as a Python example and contains no subprocess, shell, filesystem mutation, network call, or secret access. It is documentation, not a packaged runnable script.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matched Plain Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts point to plain documentation text in frontmatter and a table header. No cryptographic algorithm or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required. Treat these hits as substring matches in ordinary descriptive text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is the skill description and line 16 is a Markdown table header. Neither line defines or invokes DES, MD5, SHA1, or any other cryptographic behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent test structure, fixtures, and coverage rules across project work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise testing playbook for reliable implementation.
actual_capabilities: Classifies testing work as unit, integration, or end-to-end based on project needs.
Directs test files toward common folders such as tests/unit, tests/integration, tests/e2e, and playwright.
Promotes fixture-based test data and mock services for isolated test behavior.
Applies naming conventions for Python tests and Playwright-style E2E specifications.
Defines coverage targets for core logic, API routes, and utilities.
Summarizes CI expectations for pull requests, merges, and nightly regression runs.
limitations: The skill is a generic template and still contains placeholders for the project name and date.
It does not include executable test runners, scripts, or generated test files.
Mock service names and project-specific fixtures must be added by the user.
Coverage targets may need adjustment for legacy code or regulated systems.

use_cases.title: Standardize Test Layout
use_cases.description: Apply consistent folders, names, and coverage expectations when adding tests to an existing repository.

use_cases.title: Review Test Quality
use_cases.description: Check whether new tests use isolation, cleanup, fixtures, and the correct test category.

use_cases.title: Plan Project Test Strategy
use_cases.description: Define which workflows need unit, integration, E2E, or nightly regression coverage before implementation starts.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Test Type
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for basic testing guidance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-testing skill to decide whether this change needs unit, integration, or E2E tests. Explain the choice briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Design Fixtures
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for isolated test data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-testing skill to design fixtures for this feature. Include cleanup needs and mock service boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Review Test Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request before merging a feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-testing skill to review this test plan against naming, isolation, CI, and coverage expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Regression Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for broad quality planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-testing skill to create a regression testing strategy for this project. Separate PR, merge, and nightly coverage.

output_examples.input: A user checkout flow needs validation after a payment change.
output_examples.output: Recommended E2E coverage for the main checkout path, integration tests for payment service boundaries, and fixture cleanup after orders.

output_examples.input: A utility function was refactored without changing behavior.
output_examples.output: Recommended unit tests focused on inputs, outputs, edge cases, and fast isolated execution under the utility coverage target.

output_examples.input: A pull request adds database-backed order creation.
output_examples.output: Recommended integration tests using sample user and order fixtures, with cleanup and coverage checks for API routes.

best_practices: Pick the narrowest test type that proves the required behavior.
Use fixtures and mocks to keep tests isolated and repeatable.
Verify cleanup, naming, and coverage expectations before merging.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on timing-dependent or order-dependent tests.
Do not hardcode test data when reusable fixtures are available.
Do not skip cleanup for database, browser, or mock service state.

faq.question: Does this skill create tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance that an AI assistant can use when planning, writing, or reviewing tests.

faq.question: Which test types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, fixtures, mocks, naming, coverage, and CI expectations.

faq.question: Can it work with Playwright?
faq.answer: Yes. It references Playwright-style E2E test locations and specification naming patterns.

faq.question: Does it include project-specific mocks?
faq.answer: No. The mock services section is a placeholder that teams must customize for their repository.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file contains guidance text and examples, with no executable commands or network behavior.

faq.question: Can coverage targets be changed?
faq.answer: Yes. The listed targets are defaults and should be adjusted to match project risk and maturity.
